Page 1
Microsoft FY2025 Form 10-K
Condensed Summary Report
Prepared from the annual report for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2025.
Target length: approximately 30 pages.
This report condenses Microsoft’s 137-page FY2025 Form 10-K into a decision-oriented narrative. It 
preserves the filing’s core content: business model, segment structure, strategy, management 
discussion and analysis, audited financial statements, major accounting notes, principal risks, 
cybersecurity governance, legal and tax exposures, and corporate governance disclosures.
Source basis: the user-provided Microsoft FY2025 Form 10-K PDF, cross-checked against Microsoft Investor 
Relations and the SEC-hosted filing for consistency.
Important note: this is a summary, not a substitute for the original filing. Figures are generally shown in U.S. dollars 
and many tables are in millions unless otherwise indicated.

Page 2
Contents
1. Executive summary
2. Company overview, strategy, and operating model
3. Segment and product portfolio review
4. Fiscal 2025 financial performance
5. Segment results and operating drivers
6. Balance sheet, liquidity, cash flow, and capital allocation
7. Major accounting notes and financial statement implications
8. Risk factors and cybersecurity posture
9. Operations, people, properties, and governance
10. Overall assessment
Appendix A. Key financial tables
Appendix B. Executive officers and selected reference data

Page 3
1. Executive summary
Microsoft entered fiscal 2025 with the scale of a mature software platform company and ended it 
looking increasingly like the infrastructure and application layer for enterprise AI. The filing 
shows a business that is simultaneously growing quickly, widening strategic control points, and 
accepting lower near-term cloud margins in exchange for heavier AI infrastructure buildout.
The year’s headline outcomes were strong. Revenue rose 15% to $281.7 billion, operating income 
rose 17% to $128.5 billion, and net income rose 16% to $101.8 billion. Microsoft Cloud revenue 
reached $168.9 billion, up 23%, while Azure and other cloud services grew 34%. The company 
continued to scale both enterprise productivity and cloud infrastructure while also benefiting from 
the integration of Activision Blizzard in gaming.
The filing also shows the cost of that growth. Capital expenditures surged, lease commitments 
expanded materially, and management explicitly acknowledged margin pressure from scaling AI 
infrastructure. In other words, Microsoft is converting a large portion of current earnings power 
into datacenters, servers, GPUs, and long-duration commercial capacity.
Several themes dominate the 10-K:
 AI is now embedded across Microsoft’s entire stack: infrastructure, developer tooling, business 
applications, productivity, search, and security.
 Commercial recurring revenue remains the core earnings engine, especially Microsoft 365 
Commercial and Azure.
 Segment performance was broad-based: all three reporting segments posted revenue and 
operating income growth.
 Capital intensity increased sharply as Microsoft expanded datacenters and finance leases to 
support AI demand.
 Tax and legal matters remain important watch items, especially the IRS transfer-pricing dispute 
and data/privacy regulation in Europe.
 Cybersecurity remains both a strategic differentiator and a material source of operational and 
reputational risk.
In strategic terms, Microsoft’s FY2025 filing reads as the report of a company trying to lead the next 
platform cycle rather than defend the previous one. It is using its installed base in Office, Windows, 
developer tools, enterprise identity, and cloud to distribute AI functionality broadly, while 
simultaneously scaling the infrastructure required to support that distribution.

Page 4
2. Company overview, strategy, and operating model
Microsoft describes itself as a technology company committed to making digital technology and AI 
available broadly and responsibly. Its mission remains to empower every person and every 
organization on the planet to achieve more. The business spans software, cloud services, devices, 
online advertising, gaming, and professional networks, but the filing increasingly frames these 
offerings as connected layers of one stack rather than isolated product lines.
Management highlights three ambitions that organize research, product 
development, and go-to-market execution:
 Reinvent productivity and business processes.
 Build the intelligent cloud and intelligent edge platform.
 Create more personal computing.
The company’s near- to medium-term opportunity is described in explicitly AI-centric terms. 
Microsoft is investing to help customers maximize digital spend through the Microsoft Cloud while 
leading the AI platform wave across solution areas. Key emphasis areas include Microsoft 365 
Copilot and agents, cloud-based AI services, workplace modernization, developer productivity, 
cybersecurity, gaming expansion, and Windows-linked consumer engagement.
The filing also emphasizes sustainability commitments announced in 2020: to become carbon 
negative, water positive, and zero waste by 2030. Microsoft notes progress but also acknowledges 
that the operating environment and the technical challenge have both evolved since the goals were 
announced.
Operating segments
Beginning in fiscal 2025, Microsoft changed its segment composition to align external reporting 
with how management runs the company internally. Most notably, the commercial components of 
Microsoft 365 were brought together in Productivity and Business Processes. The three reportable 
segments are:
 Productivity and Business Processes (PBP): Microsoft 365 Commercial, Microsoft 365 Consumer, 
LinkedIn, and Dynamics.
 Intelligent Cloud (IC): Azure and other cloud services, server products, GitHub, Nuance 
Healthcare, and enterprise/partner services.
 More Personal Computing (MPC): Windows and Devices, Gaming, and Search and news 
advertising.
Why the model works
The filing repeatedly underscores the economic logic of Microsoft’s cloud model. Management says 
the Microsoft Cloud benefits from three economies of scale: lower per-unit datacenter cost, better 
utilization through aggregated customer demand, and lower maintenance labor cost through 
multi-tenancy. In practice, this allows Microsoft to cross-sell infrastructure, productivity, security, 
developer services, and data/AI offerings into the same commercial account.

Page 5
This operating model creates unusually strong strategic linkages. Windows supports Microsoft 365 
and search distribution; Azure supports GitHub, data services, and AI platforms; identity and 
security support cloud trust; and productivity apps provide a natural channel for AI monetization. 
One of the key messages of the filing is that the stack is not just broad—it is mutually reinforcing.

Page 6
3. Segment and product portfolio review
This section summarizes what each segment contains, how management describes growth drivers, 
and where the major competitive tensions sit.
Productivity and Business Processes
PBP houses Microsoft’s productivity, communication, collaboration, business applications, and 
professional network assets. The core economic engine is Microsoft 365 Commercial, which 
combines Office, Windows, Enterprise Mobility + Security, and related cloud services. Growth 
depends on seat expansion, higher revenue per user, deeper penetration of small and medium 
businesses and frontline workers, and monetization of AI-enabled scenarios through Copilot and 
related tools.
On the consumer side, Microsoft 365 Consumer remains a subscription-led productivity and 
storage offering supported by products such as Outlook.com and OneDrive. LinkedIn provides 
monetized professional network products across talent, marketing, premium subscriptions, and 
sales solutions. Dynamics contributes ERP, CRM, Power Apps, and Power Automate capabilities, 
making this segment broader than classic office software and more comparable to an integrated 
work platform.
Competition in this segment comes from productivity suites, collaboration platforms, AI-first 
workflow tools, business applications vendors, and niche security or identity vendors. Microsoft’s 
defense is integration, enterprise trust, cross-platform presence, and installed-base leverage.
Intelligent Cloud
Intelligent Cloud contains Azure, server products, developer tooling, GitHub, Nuance Healthcare, 
enterprise support, and partner services. This is the segment most visibly tied to the AI 
infrastructure cycle. Azure is described as a comprehensive cloud platform spanning compute, 
networking, storage, data, AI, machine learning, cognitive services, IoT, and app services. Microsoft 
also emphasizes Azure AI Foundry as a unified platform to design, customize, and manage AI 
applications and agents.
GitHub deepens developer ecosystem control, while Nuance adds healthcare AI capabilities. 
Traditional server products still matter, but the filing makes clear that customer shift toward cloud 
offerings continues to pressure on-premises transactional revenue. Competitive threats here come 
from hyperscale cloud providers, open-source alternatives, and specialist cybersecurity and 
analytics vendors.
More Personal Computing
MPC brings together Windows, Surface and accessories, Xbox hardware, Xbox content and 
services, and Search and news advertising through Bing, Edge, Copilot, and affiliated properties. 
This segment is more cyclical and consumer-exposed than the other two, but still strategically 
important because it controls major distribution endpoints.
Windows OEM revenue depends on PC market conditions, device mix, AI PCs, OEM inventory, 
pricing, and supply-chain availability. Gaming is now a scaled content-and-subscription business

Page 7
supported by first-party studios, Game Pass, cloud gaming, and the Activision Blizzard acquisition. 
Search and news advertising is positioned around user acquisition and engagement via Bing, Edge, 
and Copilot. Competition remains intense, particularly from alternative device ecosystems, 
entertainment platforms, console rivals, and major search and ad platforms.

Page 8
4. Fiscal 2025 financial performance
Fiscal 2025 was a year of strong top-line and operating growth, led by cloud and AI demand, 
continued Microsoft 365 momentum, strong Azure consumption, improved search economics, and 
incremental contribution from gaming content and services.
Table 1. Consolidated summary results
Metric FY2025 FY2024 Change
Revenue $281,724 $245,122 +15%
Gross margin $193,893 $171,008 +13%
Operating income $128,528 $109,433 +17%
Net income $101,832 $88,136 +16%
Diluted EPS $13.64 $11.80 +16%
Amounts in millions except per-share data.
Revenue growth of $36.6 billion was broad-based, but two engines were especially important: 
Azure within Intelligent Cloud and Microsoft 365 Commercial cloud within Productivity and 
Business Processes. Gross margin increased almost $23 billion, although gross margin percentage 
declined slightly because the mix shifted toward more infrastructure-heavy cloud activity and 
because Microsoft is scaling AI capacity ahead of demand.
Operating expenses increased only 6%, which is slower than revenue growth. That operating 
leverage allowed operating income to grow faster than revenue despite the pressure on cloud 
margin percentage. Research and development, sales and marketing, and gaming-related costs all 
rose, but the company still expanded profitability materially.
Table 2. Revenue mix by type
Revenue type FY2025 FY2024 Change
Product $63,946 $64,773 -1%
Service and other $217,778 $180,349 +21%
Total $281,724 $245,122 +15%
The filing highlights the company’s structural shift toward service and cloud revenue.
The mix shift is strategically important. Product revenue was roughly flat to slightly down, while 
service and other revenue rose sharply. This reinforces that Microsoft’s earnings profile now 
depends primarily on subscriptions, cloud consumption, support, and online services rather than 
packaged software or device unit shipments.
From a profitability perspective, FY2025 gross margin was about 68.8% of revenue, operating 
margin about 45.6%, and net margin about 36.1%. Those remain exceptionally strong at Microsoft’s 
scale, even after absorbing the cost of aggressive AI infrastructure expansion.

Page 9
5. Segment results and operating drivers
Table 3. Segment revenue and operating income
Segment Revenue FY2025 Revenue growth Operating income 
FY2025 OI growth
Productivity & 
Business Processes $120,810 +13% $69,773 +17%
Intelligent Cloud $106,265 +21% $44,589 +18%
More Personal 
Computing $54,649 +7% $14,166 +18%
Total $281,724 +15% $128,528 +17%
Productivity and Business Processes
PBP remained Microsoft’s largest segment by revenue and its most profitable by absolute operating 
income. Revenue increased $14.0 billion to $120.8 billion. The largest contributor was Microsoft 
365 Commercial products and cloud services, which grew $10.8 billion, or 14%. Microsoft 365 
Commercial cloud revenue grew 15%, supported by 6% seat growth, especially among small and 
medium businesses and frontline worker offerings, as well as higher revenue per user.
Microsoft 365 Consumer products and cloud services rose 11%, helped by subscriber growth and a 
January 2025 price increase. Consumer subscribers reached 89.0 million. LinkedIn revenue rose 
9% with growth across all lines of business. Dynamics products and cloud services grew 15%, with 
Dynamics 365 up 19%.
Operating income increased 17% to $69.8 billion. Management says cost of revenue rose due to 
Microsoft 365 Commercial cloud growth, while gross margin percentage decreased slightly because 
of AI infrastructure scaling. Even so, efficiency gains and scale in the cloud business allowed strong 
profit expansion.
Intelligent Cloud
Intelligent Cloud was the fastest-growing segment in absolute dollars. Revenue rose $18.8 billion, 
or 21%, to $106.3 billion. Server products and cloud services rose 23%, driven by Azure and other 
cloud services growth of 34%. Azure growth was fueled by demand across the portfolio rather than 
one isolated workload, reflecting both traditional cloud migration and AI-related consumption.
The flip side was cost intensity. Cost of revenue rose 36%, and gross margin percentage decreased 
because Microsoft is scaling AI infrastructure. This is one of the most important economic 
messages in the 10-K: Azure is growing extremely fast, but the company is spending heavily to 
preserve capacity leadership. Operating income still rose 18% to $44.6 billion, showing that the 
segment remains highly profitable despite margin pressure.
More Personal Computing
MPC revenue rose $3.8 billion, or 7%, to $54.6 billion. The drivers were mixed. Windows and 
Devices rose only 2%, as Windows OEM growth was partly offset by declining Devices revenue.

Page 10
Gaming revenue rose 9%, driven by 16% growth in Xbox content and services, reflecting the 
impact of Activision Blizzard and Game Pass, while Xbox hardware revenue fell 25% on lower 
console volume. Search and news advertising rose 13%, and search and news advertising 
excluding traffic acquisition costs rose 20%, driven by higher search volume and higher revenue 
per search.
Operating income increased 18% to $14.2 billion. This matters because it shows Microsoft is not 
only growing the consumer/endpoint side of the portfolio, but also translating that growth into 
better economics—especially in search and advertising—despite the weaker hardware mix.
Table 4. Operating expenses
Expense line FY2025 FY2024 Change
Research & development $32,488 $29,510 +10%
Sales & marketing $25,654 $24,456 +5%
General & administrative $7,223 $7,609 -5%
R&D rose primarily because of investments in cloud and AI engineering and the impact of 
Activision Blizzard. Sales and marketing also increased, but more slowly than revenue. General 
and administrative expense decreased modestly. The overall picture is disciplined expense growth 
in support functions combined with aggressive spending where management sees durable 
strategic return: AI, cloud engineering, gaming content, and sales capacity.

Page 11
6. Balance sheet, liquidity, cash flow, and capital allocation
Table 5. Balance sheet highlights
Balance sheet item June 30, 2025 June 30, 2024
Cash and cash equivalents $30,242 $18,315
Short-term investments $64,323 $57,228
Total cash, cash equivalents, and ST 
investments $94,565 $75,543
Accounts receivable $69,905 $56,924
Property and equipment, net $204,966 $135,591
Goodwill $119,509 $119,220
Intangible assets, net $22,604 $27,597
Total assets $619,003 $512,163
Total liabilities $275,524 $243,686
Total stockholders’ equity $343,479 $268,477
The balance sheet expanded materially in FY2025. The most important changes were the rise in 
cash and short-term investments, the large increase in property and equipment, and continued 
elevation of goodwill and intangibles following the Activision Blizzard acquisition. Net property 
and equipment rose nearly $69.4 billion year over year, reflecting unprecedented infrastructure 
buildout.
Liquidity remained very strong. Management states that existing cash, short-term investments, 
operating cash flow, and access to capital markets are expected to be sufficient to fund operations, 
dividends, repurchases, debt maturities, material capital expenditures, and tax obligations for at 
least the next 12 months and for the foreseeable future.
Table 6. Cash flow highlights
Cash flow item FY2025 FY2024
Net cash from operations $136,162 $118,548
Net cash used in investing ($72,599) ($96,970)
Net cash used in financing ($51,699) ($37,757)
Capital expenditures (property & 
equipment additions) ($64,551) ($44,477)
Cash dividends paid ($24,082) ($21,771)
Common stock repurchased ($18,420) ($17,254)
Ending cash and cash equivalents $30,242 $18,315
Operating cash flow of $136.2 billion was extremely strong and rose by $17.6 billion year over year. 
The company converted accounting earnings into cash efficiently, supported by high-margin

Page 12
recurring revenue and the scale of commercial contracts. The dominant use of that cash was 
investing activity, especially datacenter and infrastructure buildout.
Capital expenditures reached $64.6 billion, up sharply from $44.5 billion. This is one of the clearest 
quantitative signals in the report: Microsoft is in a capital deployment phase to support cloud and 
AI demand. The company is also using finance leases extensively, so cash capex understates the 
full infrastructure commitment.
Shareholder returns
Microsoft continued to return substantial capital to shareholders. During FY2025 it paid $24.1 
billion in cash dividends and spent $18.4 billion repurchasing common stock through open-market 
repurchases. Dividends declared totaled $24.7 billion, or $3.32 per share. A new $60 billion 
repurchase authorization approved in September 2024 commenced in April 2025 after the prior 
authorization was completed; $57.3 billion remained available at June 30, 2025.
Table 7. Selected contractual obligations as of June 30, 2025
Obligation 2026 Thereafter Total
Long-term debt principal $3,000 $46,206 $49,206
Long-term debt interest $1,509 $25,527 $27,036
Construction 
commitments $26,859 $5,290 $32,149
Operating & finance 
leases incl. interest $12,798 $165,903 $178,701
Purchase commitments $103,940 $6,013 $109,953
Total $148,106 $248,939 $397,045
These obligations confirm that Microsoft’s AI and cloud expansion is not just an income-statement 
story but a multi-year capital commitment story. Leases, construction commitments, and purchase 
commitments together amount to a very large future funding requirement, although one 
comfortably supported by current cash generation.

Page 13
7. Major accounting notes and financial statement 
implications
The notes to the financial statements reveal where management believes investors need additional 
context beyond the headline statements. Several notes are especially important for understanding 
Microsoft’s economics in FY2025.
Revenue recognition, unearned revenue, and remaining performance 
obligations
Unearned revenue ended FY2025 at $67.3 billion. Revenue allocated to remaining performance 
obligations reached $375 billion, of which $368 billion was commercial. Microsoft expects to 
recognize roughly 40% of total remaining performance obligations over the next 12 months, with 
the rest thereafter. This provides substantial visibility into future commercial revenue, though the 
timing varies across subscriptions, licenses, and consumption-based services.
This large remaining performance obligation base is a key feature of Microsoft’s model. It reflects 
the scale of multi-year commercial agreements and supports the view that revenue quality is high, 
even if some businesses—especially cloud consumption—remain demand sensitive.
Business combinations: Activision Blizzard
Microsoft completed the Activision Blizzard acquisition in October 2023 for $75.4 billion, primarily 
in cash. The purchase price allocation includes $51.0 billion of goodwill and $22.0 billion of 
identifiable intangible assets. Management says the acquisition is intended to accelerate growth in 
gaming across mobile, PC, console, and cloud gaming. Activision Blizzard is reported within More 
Personal Computing.
The significance is twofold. First, it explains much of the elevated goodwill and intangible asset 
base. Second, it contributed to gaming growth in FY2025, especially Xbox content and services. The 
acquisition therefore remains both a balance-sheet event and an operating event.
Goodwill and intangible assets
Total goodwill ended FY2025 at $119.5 billion, with $62.4 billion allocated to More Personal 
Computing, $31.5 billion to Productivity and Business Processes, and $25.7 billion to Intelligent 
Cloud. Microsoft reported no goodwill impairment in the annual tests for 2025, 2024, or 2023.
Net intangible assets were $22.6 billion at June 30, 2025. Amortization expense was $6.0 billion in 
FY2025, up from $4.8 billion in FY2024, and future amortization remains meaningful: $4.6 billion is 
expected in FY2026 alone. This is a non-trivial drag on reported earnings but largely reflects 
acquisition accounting rather than deterioration in the core business.
Debt and financing structure
Microsoft ended FY2025 with no commercial paper outstanding, versus $6.7 billion a year earlier. 
Total face value of long-term debt was $49.2 billion, and carrying value of total debt was $43.2 
billion after discounts, hedge adjustments, and exchange-related premiums. Current portion of 
long-term debt was $3.0 billion and long-term debt was $40.2 billion.

Page 14
The debt load remains very manageable relative to cash, investments, operating cash flow, and 
overall market position. Microsoft’s use of debt appears opportunistic and strategic rather than 
balance-sheet constrained.
Leases and off-balance-sheet-like infrastructure intensity
Lease disclosures are particularly important in FY2025 because they illuminate Microsoft’s 
infrastructure scaling strategy. Operating lease liabilities totaled $22.9 billion and finance lease 
liabilities totaled $46.2 billion. In addition, Microsoft disclosed $92.7 billion of additional leases, 
primarily for datacenters, that had not yet commenced as of June 30, 2025. These will commence 
between FY2026 and FY2031.
This disclosure is one of the strongest signals in the filing that Microsoft is locking in long-duration 
capacity for AI and cloud workloads. It also means investors should evaluate capital intensity using a 
broader lens than cash capex alone.
Taxes
Income before tax reached $123.6 billion and provision for income taxes was $21.8 billion, yielding 
an effective rate of 17.6%. The rate remained below the U.S. statutory level mainly because a large 
share of foreign income is generated through Ireland at lower tax rates. Management also 
discussed Pillar Two global minimum tax rules and said it does not currently estimate a material 
financial statement impact, though the company continues monitoring implementation across 
jurisdictions.
The tax footnote also contains one of the filing’s largest specific dispute exposures: the IRS is 
seeking an additional tax payment of $28.9 billion plus penalties and interest in connection with 
transfer-pricing issues for tax years 2004 to 2013. Microsoft says it disagrees and will vigorously 
contest the proposed adjustments. It also says current allowances for income tax contingencies are 
adequate.
Investments, derivatives, and market risk
Microsoft’s investment portfolio is primarily high-quality fixed income and liquidity-oriented 
holdings, supplemented by equity and other investments. Market risk disclosures show sensitivity 
to foreign exchange, interest rates, credit spreads, and equity prices. Management also notes that 
other income (expense), net primarily reflects recognized losses on equity method investments, 
including OpenAI, in FY2025. That point matters because it shows that strategic partnerships can 
create volatility outside core operating income.
Contingencies
The contingencies note mentions the Irish Data Protection Commission matter involving LinkedIn 
targeted advertising practices under GDPR. Microsoft states that LinkedIn appealed the final 
decision in November 2024 and that the next hearing is scheduled for December 2025. Beyond 
specific matters, aggregate legal liabilities accrued were $541 million, and adverse outcomes 
beyond recorded amounts that are reasonably possible were estimated at roughly $600 million in 
aggregate.

Page 15
8. Risk factors and cybersecurity posture
Microsoft’s risk disclosures are extensive but can be grouped into six broad categories: strategic 
and competitive risks, risks related to the evolution of the business, cybersecurity/data 
privacy/platform abuse risks, operational risks, legal/regulatory/litigation risks, and intellectual 
property/general risks.
Strategic and competitive risks
 Competition is intense across productivity software, cloud platforms, AI services, devices, 
gaming ecosystems, and digital advertising.
 Platform-based ecosystems create network effects, but competing ecosystems can restrict 
application distribution, weaken developer support, or reduce monetization opportunities.
 Business model competition includes free or ad-supported services, open-source alternatives, 
vertically integrated rivals, and low-cost operating system or platform providers.
 Microsoft’s shift toward cloud and AI services requires continued execution and heavy 
infrastructure spending; failure to monetize at scale could pressure returns.
Risks related to business evolution
 Large investments in new products, services, hardware, and AI may not earn expected returns.
 Acquisitions, partnerships, and alliances—including large strategic deals—may create 
integration, compliance, valuation, or execution risks.
 Data handling practices, regulatory scrutiny, and public perceptions of product misuse or 
mismanagement could reduce adoption.
Cybersecurity, data privacy, and platform abuse
Cybersecurity is unusually central in Microsoft’s 10-K because the company is both a major 
technology provider and a high-profile cyber target. The filing explicitly references prior incidents, 
including a nation-state associated threat actor that used a password-spray attack to compromise a 
legacy test account and gain access to Microsoft email accounts and some source code repositories. 
Microsoft warns that such incidents can harm reputation, customer relationships, and results of 
operations.
The company says it launched the Secure Future Initiative in November 2023 and expanded it in 
May 2024. The initiative is built around three principles: Secure by Design, Secure by Default, and 
Secure Operations. Microsoft says it assesses alignment to the NIST Cybersecurity Framework 
annually, undertakes tabletop exercises, production environment testing, red teaming, 
vulnerability testing, employee training, and simulated phishing education. The company also 
states that it uses insights from 84 trillion signals per day in its security operations.
Governance of cybersecurity is formalized. The board oversees cybersecurity risk, the CISO reports 
to the EVP of Cloud + AI, and a Cybersecurity Governance Council of deputy CISOs approves the 
enterprise risk assessment process, determines risk levels and mitigations, reviews NIST 
alignment, and supports compliance with cybersecurity regulations.

Page 16
Operational risks
 Cloud and AI growth depend on datacenter land, energy, networking, servers, GPUs, and 
related supply-chain resilience.
 Some server and device components have few qualified suppliers, creating concentration risk.
 Software quality, service reliability, outages, or patching failures could disrupt customers and 
magnify liability or reputational damage.
Legal and regulatory risks
 Microsoft operates under evolving laws and regulations related to privacy, advertising, data 
storage, competition, digital markets, content, and AI.
 Regulatory actions in major jurisdictions, particularly the EU, can require operational changes 
and raise compliance costs.
 Tax disputes, antitrust or platform-related investigations, and data protection enforcement 
remain meaningful watch points.
Bottom line on risk posture
The 10-K does not suggest fragility. Instead, it depicts a company with enormous financial 
resilience that nevertheless faces real execution and trust risks because of its scale, centrality, and 
exposure to the AI and cloud infrastructure cycle. For investors, the most important risks to 
monitor are cybersecurity, AI-related regulation and monetization, infrastructure cost intensity, 
tax disputes, and the ability to maintain high trust while moving quickly.

Page 17
9. Operations, people, properties, and governance
Human capital
As of June 30, 2025, Microsoft employed approximately 228,000 full-time employees, including 
125,000 in the United States and 103,000 internationally. Of these, approximately 89,000 were in 
operations, 80,000 in product research and development, 44,000 in sales and marketing, and 15,000 
in general and administration.
Management says compensation, benefits, wellbeing programs, pay equity monitoring, continuous 
learning, and employee listening systems are important to attracting and retaining talent. The 
filing also emphasizes that security responsibility is embedded across teams and functions, not 
isolated within one technical group.
Operations and properties
Microsoft maintains regional operations service centers across the Americas, Asia Pacific, Europe, 
and the Middle East. It operates datacenters in these regions and says location and capacity 
decisions increasingly reflect AI-service demand. The company stresses dependencies on permitted 
land, predictable energy, networking supplies, and server components including GPUs.
On properties, Microsoft reports roughly 97 million square feet of total owned and leased property 
worldwide, of which 57 million square feet are in the U.S. and 40 million internationally. In King 
County, Washington, it has approximately 15 million square feet, including about 12 million square 
feet of owned space on roughly 530 acres at headquarters.
Research and development
Microsoft continues to invest heavily in both fundamental and applied R&D across cloud, AI, 
devices, security, operating systems, and digital work/life experiences. The report frames in-house 
development as a source of competitive advantage because it preserves product differentiation, 
control over implementation, and freedom to prioritize modifications and enhancements.
Distribution, sales, and licensing
The filing outlines a broad commercial distribution model spanning direct sales, enterprise 
agreements, Microsoft Customer Agreements, reseller channels, cloud solution providers, OEM 
relationships, and online direct channels. This matters because Microsoft’s performance is not 
dependent on a single sales motion. Its commercial engine combines long-cycle enterprise 
contracting with self-serve and partner-led distribution.
The company also uses several licensing frameworks—Enterprise Agreement, Microsoft Customer 
Agreement, Microsoft Online Subscription Agreement, Microsoft Products and Services Agreement, 
Open Value, and Select Plus—to match contract form to customer size and deployment model. The 
result is high commercial flexibility with strong lock-in effects.
Governance and executives
The executive officers listed in the filing as of July 30, 2025 were Satya Nadella (Chairman and 
CEO), Judson Althoff (EVP and Chief Commercial Officer), Amy Coleman (EVP and Chief Human

Page 18
Resources Officer), Kathleen Hogan (EVP, Office of Strategy and Transformation), Amy Hood (EVP 
and CFO), Takeshi Numoto (EVP and Chief Marketing Officer), and Bradford Smith (Vice Chair and 
President).
The filing also states that disclosure controls and procedures were effective as of June 30, 2025. 
Management concluded internal control over financial reporting was effective under the COSO 
2013 framework, and Deloitte & Touche LLP audited internal control over financial reporting and 
the consolidated financial statements.

Page 19
10. Overall assessment
Microsoft’s FY2025 Form 10-K presents a company in a position of unusual strength. The core 
software and cloud franchise is still expanding, the productivity stack remains a major recurring-
revenue engine, Azure is capturing large-scale AI and infrastructure demand, and the company 
retains enough financial capacity to fund both exceptional shareholder returns and a historically 
large capital expansion cycle.
The most important judgment call for readers is how to interpret the trade-off between present 
margins and future strategic positioning. Microsoft is plainly spending heavily on AI capacity, 
datacenter expansion, and long-duration lease commitments. This increases cost of revenue and 
can depress cloud gross margin percentage in the near term. But management is signaling that 
capacity leadership and distribution breadth matter more than maximizing short-term margin on 
every unit of demand.
The filing also suggests that Microsoft’s competitive moat is no longer best understood as 
“Windows plus Office.” It is now the combination of commercial distribution, enterprise trust, 
identity, security, developer tools, business applications, collaboration, and cloud infrastructure—
plus the ability to insert AI across all of them. That is a much broader and more defensible control 
position.
At the same time, the report does not support complacency. The company remains exposed to cyber 
incidents, platform regulation, infrastructure bottlenecks, acquisition execution risk, and large tax 
disputes. If any of those areas go badly, Microsoft’s scale ensures the consequences could also be large. 
Still, the FY2025 filing overall reads as the report of a company with expanding strategic relevance, 
robust earnings quality, and the balance-sheet capacity to keep investing ahead of demand.

Page 20

and many tables are in millions unless otherwise indicated.

Page 3
1. Executive summary
Microsoft entered fiscal 2025 with the scale of a mature software platform company and ended it 
looking increasingly like the infrastructure and application layer for enterprise AI. The filing 
shows a business that is simultaneously growing quickly, widening strategic control points, and 
accepting lower near-term cloud margins in exchange for heavier AI infrastructure buildout.
The year’s headline outcomes were strong. Revenue rose 15% to $281.7 billion, operating income 
rose 17% to $128.5 billion, and net income rose 16% to $101.8 billion. Microsoft Cloud revenue 
reached $168.9 billion, up 23%, while Azure and other cloud services grew 34%. The company 
continued to scale both enterprise productivity and cloud infrastructure while also benefiting from 
the integration of Activision Blizzard in gaming.
The filing also shows the cost of that growth. Capital expenditures surged, lease commitments 
expanded materially, and management explicitly acknowledged margin pressure from scaling AI 
infrastructure. In other words, Microsoft is converting a large portion of current earnings power 
into datacenters, servers, GPUs, and long-duration commercial capacity.
Several themes dominate the 10-K:
 AI is now embedded across Microsoft’s entire stack: infrastructure, developer tooling, business 
applications, productivity, search, and security.
 Commercial recurring revenue remains the core earnings engine, especially Microsoft 365 
Commercial and Azure.
 Segment performance was broad-based: all three reporting segments posted revenue and 
operating income growth.
 Capital intensity increased sharply as Microsoft expanded datacenters and finance leases to 
support AI demand.
 Tax and legal matters remain important watch items, especially the IRS transfer-pricing dispute 
and data/privacy regulation in Europe.
 Cybersecurity remains both a strategic differentiator and a material source of operational and 
reputational risk.
In strategic terms, Microsoft’s FY2025 filing reads as the report of a company trying to lead the next 
platform cycle rather than defend the previous one. It is using its installed base in Office, Windows, 
developer tools, enterprise identity, and cloud to distribute AI functionality broadly, while 
simultaneously scaling the infrastructure required to support that distribution.

[PAGE_MARKER_4]

Page 4
2. Company overview, strategy, and operating model
Microsoft describes itself as a technology company committed to making digital technology and AI 
available broadly and responsibly. Its mission remains to empower every person and every 
organization on the planet to achieve more. The business spans software, cloud services, devices, 
online advertising, gaming, and professional networks, but the filing increasingly frames these 
offerings as connected layers of one stack rather than isolated product lines.
Management highlights three ambitions that organize research, product 
development, and go-to-market execution:
 Reinvent productivity and business processes.
 Build the intelligent cloud and intelligent edge platform.
 Create more personal computing.
The company’s near- to medium-term opportunity is described in explicitly AI-centric terms. 
Microsoft is investing to help customers maximize digital spend through the Microsoft Cloud while 
leading the AI platform wave across solution areas. Key emphasis areas include Microsoft 365 
Copilot and agents, cloud-based AI services, workplace modernization, developer productivity, 
cybersecurity, gaming expansion, and Windows-linked consumer engagement.
The filing also emphasizes sustainability commitments announced in 2020: to become carbon 
negative, water positive, and zero waste by 2030. Microsoft notes progress but also acknowledges 
that the operating environment and the technical challenge have both evolved since the goals were 
announced.
Operating segments
Beginning in fiscal 2025, Microsoft changed its segment composition to align external reporting 
with how management runs the company internally. Most notably, the commercial components of 
Microsoft 365 were brought together in Productivity and Business Processes. The three reportable 
segments are:
 Productivity and Business Processes (PBP): Microsoft 365 Commercial, Microsoft 365 Consumer, 
LinkedIn, and Dynamics.
 Intelligent Cloud (IC): Azure and other cloud services, server products, GitHub, Nuance 
Healthcare, and enterprise/partner services.
 More Personal Computing (MPC): Windows and Devices, Gaming, and Search and news 
advertising.
Why the model works
The filing repeatedly underscores the economic logic of Microsoft’s cloud model. Management says 
the Microsoft Cloud benefits from three economies of scale: lower per-unit datacenter cost, better 
utilization through aggregated customer demand, and lower maintenance labor cost through 
multi-tenancy. In practice, this allows Microsoft to cross-sell infrastructure, productivity, security, 
developer services, and data/AI offerings into the same commercial account.

[PAGE_MARKER_5]

Page 5
This operating model creates unusually strong strategic linkages. Windows supports Microsoft 365 
and search distribution; Azure supports GitHub, data services, and AI platforms; identity and 
security support cloud trust; and productivity apps provide a natural channel for AI monetization. 
One of the key messages of the filing is that the stack is not just broad—it is mutually reinforcing.

[PAGE_MARKER_6]

Page 6
3. Segment and product portfolio review
This section summarizes what each segment contains, how management describes growth drivers, 
and where the major competitive tensions sit.
Productivity and Business Processes
PBP houses Microsoft’s productivity, communication, collaboration, business applications, and 
professional network assets. The core economic engine is Microsoft 365 Commercial, which 
combines Office, Windows, Enterprise Mobility + Security, and related cloud services. Growth 
depends on seat expansion, higher revenue per user, deeper penetration of small and medium 
businesses and frontline workers, and monetization of AI-enabled scenarios through Copilot and 
related tools.
On the consumer side, Microsoft 365 Consumer remains a subscription-led productivity and 
storage offering supported by products such as Outlook.com and OneDrive. LinkedIn provides 
monetized professional network products across talent, marketing, premium subscriptions, and 
sales solutions. Dynamics contributes ERP, CRM, Power Apps, and Power Automate capabilities, 
making this segment broader than classic office software and more comparable to an integrated 
work platform.
Competition in this segment comes from productivity suites, collaboration platforms, AI-first 
workflow tools, business applications vendors, and niche security or identity vendors. Microsoft’s 
defense is integration, enterprise trust, cross-platform presence, and installed-base leverage.
Intelligent Cloud
Intelligent Cloud contains Azure, server products, developer tooling, GitHub, Nuance Healthcare, 
enterprise support, and partner services. This is the segment most visibly tied to the AI 
infrastructure cycle. Azure is described as a comprehensive cloud platform spanning compute, 
networking, storage, data, AI, machine learning, cognitive services, IoT, and app services. Microsoft 
also emphasizes Azure AI Foundry as a unified platform to design, customize, and manage AI 
applications and agents.
GitHub deepens developer ecosystem control, while Nuance adds healthcare AI capabilities. 
Traditional server products still matter, but the filing makes clear that customer shift toward cloud 
offerings continues to pressure on-premises transactional revenue. Competitive threats here come 
from hyperscale cloud providers, open-source alternatives, and specialist cybersecurity and 
analytics vendors.
More Personal Computing
MPC brings together Windows, Surface and accessories, Xbox hardware, Xbox content and 
services, and Search and news advertising through Bing, Edge, Copilot, and affiliated properties. 
This segment is more cyclical and consumer-exposed than the other two, but still strategically 
important because it controls major distribution endpoints.
Windows OEM revenue depends on PC market conditions, device mix, AI PCs, OEM inventory, 
pricing, and supply-chain availability. Gaming is now a scaled content-and-subscription business 

[PAGE_MARKER_7]

Page 7
supported by first-party studios, Game Pass, cloud gaming, and the Activision Blizzard acquisition. 
Search and news advertising is positioned around user acquisition and engagement via Bing, Edge, 
and Copilot. Competition remains intense, particularly from alternative device ecosystems, 
entertainment platforms, console rivals, and major search and ad platforms.

[PAGE_MARKER_8]

Page 8
4. Fiscal 2025 financial performance
Fiscal 2025 was a year of strong top-line and operating growth, led by cloud and AI demand, 
continued Microsoft 365 momentum, strong Azure consumption, improved search economics, and 
incremental contribution from gaming content and services.
Table 1. Consolidated summary results
Metric FY2025 FY2024 Change
Revenue $281,724 $245,122 +15%
Gross margin $193,893 $171,008 +13%
Operating income $128,528 $109,433 +17%
Net income $101,832 $88,136 +16%
Diluted EPS $13.64 $11.80 +16%
Amounts in millions except per-share data.
Revenue growth of $36.6 billion was broad-based, but two engines were especially important: 
Azure within Intelligent Cloud and Microsoft 365 Commercial cloud within Productivity and 
Business Processes. Gross margin increased almost $23 billion, although gross margin percentage 
declined slightly because the mix shifted toward more infrastructure-heavy cloud activity and 
because Microsoft is scaling AI capacity ahead of demand.
Operating expenses increased only 6%, which is slower than revenue growth. That operating 
leverage allowed operating income to grow faster than revenue despite the pressure on cloud 
margin percentage. Research and development, sales and marketing, and gaming-related costs all 
rose, but the company still expanded profitability materially.
Table 2. Revenue mix by type
Revenue type FY2025 FY2024 Change
Product $63,946 $64,773 -1%
Service and other $217,778 $180,349 +21%
Total $281,724 $245,122 +15%
The filing highlights the company’s structural shift toward service and cloud revenue.
The mix shift is strategically important. Product revenue was roughly flat to slightly down, while 
service and other revenue rose sharply. This reinforces that Microsoft’s earnings profile now 
depends primarily on subscriptions, cloud consumption, support, and online services rather than 
packaged software or device unit shipments.
From a profitability perspective, FY2025 gross margin was about 68.8% of revenue, operating 
margin about 45.6%, and net margin about 36.1%. Those remain exceptionally strong at Microsoft’s 
scale, even after absorbing the cost of aggressive AI infrastructure expansion.

Table 3. Segment revenue and operating income

Productivity and Business Processes
PBP remained Microsoft’s largest segment by revenue and its most profitable by absolute operating 
income. Revenue increased $14.0 billion to $120.8 billion. The largest contributor was Microsoft 
365 Commercial products and cloud services, which grew $10.8 billion, or 14%. Microsoft 365 
Commercial cloud revenue grew 15%, supported by 6% seat growth, especially among small and 
medium businesses and frontline worker offerings, as well as higher revenue per user.
Microsoft 365 Consumer products and cloud services rose 11%, helped by subscriber growth and a 
January 2025 price increase. Consumer subscribers reached 89.0 million. LinkedIn revenue rose 
9% with growth across all lines of business. Dynamics products and cloud services grew 15%, with 
Dynamics 365 up 19%.
Operating income increased 17% to $69.8 billion. Management says cost of revenue rose due to 
Microsoft 365 Commercial cloud growth, while gross margin percentage decreased slightly because 
of AI infrastructure scaling. Even so, efficiency gains and scale in the cloud business allowed strong 
profit expansion.
Intelligent Cloud
Intelligent Cloud was the fastest-growing segment in absolute dollars. Revenue rose $18.8 billion, 
or 21%, to $106.3 billion. Server products and cloud services rose 23%, driven by Azure and other 
cloud services growth of 34%. Azure growth was fueled by demand across the portfolio rather than 
one isolated workload, reflecting both traditional cloud migration and AI-related consumption.
The flip side was cost intensity. Cost of revenue rose 36%, and gross margin percentage decreased 
because Microsoft is scaling AI infrastructure. This is one of the most important economic 
messages in the 10-K: Azure is growing extremely fast, but the company is spending heavily to 
preserve capacity leadership. Operating income still rose 18% to $44.6 billion, showing that the 
segment remains highly profitable despite margin pressure.
More Personal Computing
MPC revenue rose $3.8 billion, or 7%, to $54.6 billion. The drivers were mixed. Windows and 
Devices rose only 2%, as Windows OEM growth was partly offset by declining Devices revenue. 

[PAGE_MARKER_10]

Page 10
Gaming revenue rose 9%, driven by 16% growth in Xbox content and services, reflecting the 
impact of Activision Blizzard and Game Pass, while Xbox hardware revenue fell 25% on lower 
console volume. Search and news advertising rose 13%, and search and news advertising 
excluding traffic acquisition costs rose 20%, driven by higher search volume and higher revenue 
per search.
Operating income increased 18% to $14.2 billion. This matters because it shows Microsoft is not 
only growing the consumer/endpoint side of the portfolio, but also translating that growth into 
better economics—especially in search and advertising—despite the weaker hardware mix.

Activision Blizzard. Sales and marketing also increased, but more slowly than revenue. General 
and administrative expense decreased modestly. The overall picture is disciplined expense growth 
in support functions combined with aggressive spending where management sees durable 
strategic return: AI, cloud engineering, gaming content, and sales capacity.

Liquidity remained very strong. Management states that existing cash, short-term investments,

7. Major accounting notes and financial statement 
implications
The notes to the financial statements reveal where management believes investors need additional 
context beyond the headline statements. Several notes are especially important for understanding 
Microsoft’s economics in FY2025.
Revenue recognition, unearned revenue, and remaining performance 
obligations
Unearned revenue ended FY2025 at $67.3 billion. Revenue allocated to remaining performance 
obligations reached $375 billion, of which $368 billion was commercial. Microsoft expects to 
recognize roughly 40% of total remaining performance obligations over the next 12 months, with 
the rest thereafter. This provides substantial visibility into future commercial revenue, though the 
timing varies across subscriptions, licenses, and consumption-based services.
This large remaining performance obligation base is a key feature of Microsoft’s model. It reflects 
the scale of multi-year commercial agreements and supports the view that revenue quality is high, 
even if some businesses—especially cloud consumption—remain demand sensitive.
Business combinations: Activision Blizzard
Microsoft completed the Activision Blizzard acquisition in October 2023 for $75.4 billion, primarily 
in cash. The purchase price allocation includes $51.0 billion of goodwill and $22.0 billion of 
identifiable intangible assets. Management says the acquisition is intended to accelerate growth in 
gaming across mobile, PC, console, and cloud gaming. Activision Blizzard is reported within More 
Personal Computing.
The significance is twofold. First, it explains much of the elevated goodwill and intangible asset 
base. Second, it contributed to gaming growth in FY2025, especially Xbox content and services. The 
acquisition therefore remains both a balance-sheet event and an operating event.
Goodwill and intangible assets
Total goodwill ended FY2025 at $119.5 billion, with $62.4 billion allocated to More Personal 
Computing, $31.5 billion to Productivity and Business Processes, and $25.7 billion to Intelligent 
Cloud. Microsoft reported no goodwill impairment in the annual tests for 2025, 2024, or 2023.
Net intangible assets were $22.6 billion at June 30, 2025. Amortization expense was $6.0 billion in 
FY2025, up from $4.8 billion in FY2024, and future amortization remains meaningful: $4.6 billion is 
expected in FY2026 alone. This is a non-trivial drag on reported earnings but largely reflects 
acquisition accounting rather than deterioration in the core business.
Debt and financing structure
Microsoft ended FY2025 with no commercial paper outstanding, versus $6.7 billion a year earlier. 
Total face value of long-term debt was $49.2 billion, and carrying value of total debt was $43.2 
billion after discounts, hedge adjustments, and exchange-related premiums. Current portion of 
long-term debt was $3.0 billion and long-term debt was $40.2 billion.

The debt load remains very manageable relative to cash, investments, operating cash flow, and 
overall market position. Microsoft’s use of debt appears opportunistic and strategic rather than 
balance-sheet constrained.
Leases and off-balance-sheet-like infrastructure intensity
Lease disclosures are particularly important in FY2025 because they illuminate Microsoft’s 
infrastructure scaling strategy. Operating lease liabilities totaled $22.9 billion and finance lease 
liabilities totaled $46.2 billion. In addition, Microsoft disclosed $92.7 billion of additional leases, 
primarily for datacenters, that had not yet commenced as of June 30, 2025. These will commence 
between FY2026 and FY2031.
This disclosure is one of the strongest signals in the filing that Microsoft is locking in long-duration 
capacity for AI and cloud workloads. It also means investors should evaluate capital intensity using a 
broader lens than cash capex alone.
Taxes
Income before tax reached $123.6 billion and provision for income taxes was $21.8 billion, yielding 
an effective rate of 17.6%. The rate remained below the U.S. statutory level mainly because a large 
share of foreign income is generated through Ireland at lower tax rates. Management also 
discussed Pillar Two global minimum tax rules and said it does not currently estimate a material 
financial statement impact, though the company continues monitoring implementation across 
jurisdictions.
The tax footnote also contains one of the filing’s largest specific dispute exposures: the IRS is 
seeking an additional tax payment of $28.9 billion plus penalties and interest in connection with 
transfer-pricing issues for tax years 2004 to 2013. Microsoft says it disagrees and will vigorously 
contest the proposed adjustments. It also says current allowances for income tax contingencies are 
adequate.
Investments, derivatives, and market risk
Microsoft’s investment portfolio is primarily high-quality fixed income and liquidity-oriented 
holdings, supplemented by equity and other investments. Market risk disclosures show sensitivity 
to foreign exchange, interest rates, credit spreads, and equity prices. Management also notes that 
other income (expense), net primarily reflects recognized losses on equity method investments, 
including OpenAI, in FY2025. That point matters because it shows that strategic partnerships can 
create volatility outside core operating income.
Contingencies
The contingencies note mentions the Irish Data Protection Commission matter involving LinkedIn 
targeted advertising practices under GDPR. Microsoft states that LinkedIn appealed the final 
decision in November 2024 and that the next hearing is scheduled for December 2025. Beyond 
specific matters, aggregate legal liabilities accrued were $541 million, and adverse outcomes 
beyond recorded amounts that are reasonably possible were estimated at roughly $600 million in 
aggregate.

8. Risk factors and cybersecurity posture
Microsoft’s risk disclosures are extensive but can be grouped into six broad categories: strategic 
and competitive risks, risks related to the evolution of the business, cybersecurity/data 
privacy/platform abuse risks, operational risks, legal/regulatory/litigation risks, and intellectual 
property/general risks.
Strategic and competitive risks
 Competition is intense across productivity software, cloud platforms, AI services, devices, 
gaming ecosystems, and digital advertising.
 Platform-based ecosystems create network effects, but competing ecosystems can restrict 
application distribution, weaken developer support, or reduce monetization opportunities.
 Business model competition includes free or ad-supported services, open-source alternatives, 
vertically integrated rivals, and low-cost operating system or platform providers.
 Microsoft’s shift toward cloud and AI services requires continued execution and heavy 
infrastructure spending; failure to monetize at scale could pressure returns.
Risks related to business evolution
 Large investments in new products, services, hardware, and AI may not earn expected returns.
 Acquisitions, partnerships, and alliances—including large strategic deals—may create 
integration, compliance, valuation, or execution risks.
 Data handling practices, regulatory scrutiny, and public perceptions of product misuse or 
mismanagement could reduce adoption.
Cybersecurity, data privacy, and platform abuse
Cybersecurity is unusually central in Microsoft’s 10-K because the company is both a major 
technology provider and a high-profile cyber target. The filing explicitly references prior incidents, 
including a nation-state associated threat actor that used a password-spray attack to compromise a 
legacy test account and gain access to Microsoft email accounts and some source code repositories. 
Microsoft warns that such incidents can harm reputation, customer relationships, and results of 
operations.
The company says it launched the Secure Future Initiative in November 2023 and expanded it in 
May 2024. The initiative is built around three principles: Secure by Design, Secure by Default, and 
Secure Operations. Microsoft says it assesses alignment to the NIST Cybersecurity Framework 
annually, undertakes tabletop exercises, production environment testing, red teaming, 
vulnerability testing, employee training, and simulated phishing education. The company also 
states that it uses insights from 84 trillion signals per day in its security operations.
Governance of cybersecurity is formalized. The board oversees cybersecurity risk, the CISO reports 
to the EVP of Cloud + AI, and a Cybersecurity Governance Council of deputy CISOs approves the 
enterprise risk assessment process, determines risk levels and mitigations, reviews NIST 
alignment, and supports compliance with cybersecurity regulations.

[PAGE_MARKER_16]

Page 16
Operational risks
 Cloud and AI growth depend on datacenter land, energy, networking, servers, GPUs, and 
related supply-chain resilience.
 Some server and device components have few qualified suppliers, creating concentration risk.
 Software quality, service reliability, outages, or patching failures could disrupt customers and 
magnify liability or reputational damage.
Legal and regulatory risks
 Microsoft operates under evolving laws and regulations related to privacy, advertising, data 
storage, competition, digital markets, content, and AI.
 Regulatory actions in major jurisdictions, particularly the EU, can require operational changes 
and raise compliance costs.
 Tax disputes, antitrust or platform-related investigations, and data protection enforcement 
remain meaningful watch points.
Bottom line on risk posture
The 10-K does not suggest fragility. Instead, it depicts a company with enormous financial 
resilience that nevertheless faces real execution and trust risks because of its scale, centrality, and 
exposure to the AI and cloud infrastructure cycle. For investors, the most important risks to 
monitor are cybersecurity, AI-related regulation and monetization, infrastructure cost intensity, 
tax disputes, and the ability to maintain high trust while moving quickly.

9. Operations, people, properties, and governance
Human capital
As of June 30, 2025, Microsoft employed approximately 228,000 full-time employees, including 
125,000 in the United States and 103,000 internationally. Of these, approximately 89,000 were in 
operations, 80,000 in product research and development, 44,000 in sales and marketing, and 15,000 
in general and administration.
Management says compensation, benefits, wellbeing programs, pay equity monitoring, continuous 
learning, and employee listening systems are important to attracting and retaining talent. The 
filing also emphasizes that security responsibility is embedded across teams and functions, not 
isolated within one technical group.
Operations and properties
Microsoft maintains regional operations service centers across the Americas, Asia Pacific, Europe, 
and the Middle East. It operates datacenters in these regions and says location and capacity 
decisions increasingly reflect AI-service demand. The company stresses dependencies on permitted 
land, predictable energy, networking supplies, and server components including GPUs.
On properties, Microsoft reports roughly 97 million square feet of total owned and leased property 
worldwide, of which 57 million square feet are in the U.S. and 40 million internationally. In King 
County, Washington, it has approximately 15 million square feet, including about 12 million square 
feet of owned space on roughly 530 acres at headquarters.
Research and development
Microsoft continues to invest heavily in both fundamental and applied R&D across cloud, AI, 
devices, security, operating systems, and digital work/life experiences. The report frames in-house 
development as a source of competitive advantage because it preserves product differentiation, 
control over implementation, and freedom to prioritize modifications and enhancements.
Distribution, sales, and licensing
The filing outlines a broad commercial distribution model spanning direct sales, enterprise 
agreements, Microsoft Customer Agreements, reseller channels, cloud solution providers, OEM 
relationships, and online direct channels. This matters because Microsoft’s performance is not 
dependent on a single sales motion. Its commercial engine combines long-cycle enterprise 
contracting with self-serve and partner-led distribution.
The company also uses several licensing frameworks—Enterprise Agreement, Microsoft Customer 
Agreement, Microsoft Online Subscription Agreement, Microsoft Products and Services Agreement, 
Open Value, and Select Plus—to match contract form to customer size and deployment model. The 
result is high commercial flexibility with strong lock-in effects.
Governance and executives
The executive officers listed in the filing as of July 30, 2025 were Satya Nadella (Chairman and 
CEO), Judson Althoff (EVP and Chief Commercial Officer), Amy Coleman (EVP and Chief Human

10. Overall assessment
Microsoft’s FY2025 Form 10-K presents a company in a position of unusual strength. The core 
software and cloud franchise is still expanding, the productivity stack remains a major recurring-
revenue engine, Azure is capturing large-scale AI and infrastructure demand, and the company 
retains enough financial capacity to fund both exceptional shareholder returns and a historically 
large capital expansion cycle.
The most important judgment call for readers is how to interpret the trade-off between present 
margins and future strategic positioning. Microsoft is plainly spending heavily on AI capacity, 
datacenter expansion, and long-duration lease commitments. This increases cost of revenue and 
can depress cloud gross margin percentage in the near term. But management is signaling that 
capacity leadership and distribution breadth matter more than maximizing short-term margin on 
every unit of demand.
The filing also suggests that Microsoft’s competitive moat is no longer best understood as 
“Windows plus Office.” It is now the combination of commercial distribution, enterprise trust, 
identity, security, developer tools, business applications, collaboration, and cloud infrastructure—
plus the ability to insert AI across all of them. That is a much broader and more defensible control 
position.
At the same time, the report does not support complacency. The company remains exposed to cyber 
incidents, platform regulation, infrastructure bottlenecks, acquisition execution risk, and large tax 
disputes. If any of those areas go badly, Microsoft’s scale ensures the consequences could also be large. 
Still, the FY2025 filing overall reads as the report of a company with expanding strategic relevance, 
robust earnings quality, and the balance-sheet capacity to keep investing ahead of demand.
